name: Parker Peak
short_description: Parker Peak in the Black Hills region offers rockhounding for quartz and agate specimens on public land with gentle terrain and year-round access.
description:
Parker Peak sits in the northern Black Hills of South Dakota, part of the ancient Precambrian granite and metamorphic complex that underlies this iconic mountain range. The area around Parker Peak is characterized by gentle slopes at 4416 feet elevation, making it accessible for rockhounds of varying experience levels. The Black Hills formed during the Laramide Orogeny and contain abundant quartz veins, secondary agate deposits, and chalcedony specimens that weathered from the parent rock formations. This location offers diverse collecting opportunities in a scenic landscape where you can search weathered outcrops, talus slopes, and washed material for quality specimens.

The geology here reflects the collision and uplift that created the Black Hills, with Precambrian schist, granite, and pegmatite bodies hosting excellent quartz crystals and microcrystalline varieties. Agate forms in vugs and cavities within the metamorphic host rock, while chalcedony commonly lines fractures and weathered zones. The gentle slope makes walking and specimen hunting straightforward, though you should expect rocky footing typical of mountain terrain. Fall River County rockhounds frequently find transparent quartz points, banded agate nodules, and translucent chalcedony pieces in the loose material and exposed rock faces.

Parker Peak is best visited during warmer months when weather is stable and access roads are passable. The area experiences moderate Black Hills weather with cool mornings and mild afternoons. Bring plenty of water, sun protection, and wear sturdy hiking boots with good ankle support. The site rewards patient collectors who carefully examine weathered zones and examine talus accumulations at the base of outcrops.
history: The Black Hills Precambrian complex underwent intense metamorphism and granite intrusion during the Archean and Proterozoic eons, creating the fractured, mineralized rock that hosts quartz veins and agate deposits found today. Early prospectors in the Black Hills region discovered abundant quartz and chalcedony specimens, and the area has been a popular rockhounding destination for over a century.
tips: Search the talus slopes and wash areas at the base of rocky outcrops where quartz and agate specimens naturally accumulate and concentrate. Use a small pry bar or chisel to carefully extract specimens from weathered rock faces and vugs, and always look for translucent to transparent quartz points and banded agate nodules that indicate mineralized zones. Examine areas where water has weathered the granite host rock, as these zones are enriched in secondary quartz and chalcedony. Early morning light helps you spot the glassy luster of quartz and translucent agate against gray metamorphic rock.
terrain_notes: Elevation 4416 feet with gentle slopes throughout the collecting area. Terrain is typical Black Hills granite and metamorphic rock, with scattered pines and alpine vegetation. Rocky footing requires sturdy hiking boots, but walking distances from parking are manageable. Loose stone and weathered talus is common; pace yourself carefully on slopes.
best_season: June - September. Higher elevation (4400+ feet) means winter snow and cold make earlier and later months difficult. Summer and early fall offer stable weather, passable roads, and moderate temperatures ideal for sustained rockhounding.
written_directions: From Hot Springs, South Dakota (nearest significant town, approximately 25 miles southeast), take US 385 north toward the Black Hills. Continue on US 385 or transition to Forest Service Road 130 heading north and east into the Black Hills proper, following signs toward the Parker Peak area. The final approach is typically via rough gravel forest service roads; confirm current access with the Black Hills National Forest office in Hot Springs before traveling, as forest service roads can be seasonally closed. GPS navigation to 43.405267, -103.675333 is essential; the site is accessed via foot from a parking pullout on forest service land.
road_conditions: Rough gravel forest service roads with potential washboard and ruts. High-clearance vehicle recommended. Confirm road passability with Black Hills National Forest before traveling.
parking_notes: Small parking pullout accessed from forest service road. Undesignated parking typical of national forest land; capacity varies. Arrive early in season for reliable access.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. Collect only hand-portable specimens. Respect private property boundaries. Leave no excavations or permanent marks. Pack out all collection bags and trash.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
Hand tools only
Pack out trash and collection bags
Respect private property
No permanent damage to rock faces
nearest_services: Hot Springs, South Dakota has gas, food, and lodging approximately 25 miles south. Custer, South Dakota (35 miles south) offers additional services.
accessibility_notes: Moderate terrain with rocky footing and gentle slopes. Uneven ground throughout collecting area requires sturdy hiking boots and reasonable fitness. Not suitable for wheelchair access. Walking distances from parking to productive areas range from 0.5 to 2 miles depending on exact parking location. Some scrambling over talus may be necessary.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find rockhounding at Parker Peak in South Dakota?
faq.answer: Parker Peak is known for high-quality quartz specimens including transparent points and clusters, banded agate nodules, and chalcedony. The Precambrian granite and metamorphic host rock at this 4416-foot elevation site concentrates these secondary minerals in weathered outcrops and talus slopes, making it an excellent location for collectors seeking Black Hills gemstones.

faq.question: Is Parker Peak rockhounding site free and do I need a permit?
faq.answer: Yes, Parker Peak is on public national forest land and is free to rockhound with no permit required. You must follow personal use collection rules: 25 lbs per day maximum, hand tools only, no commercial collection, and no motorized excavation. This makes it an accessible and affordable option for South Dakota rockhounders.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding at Parker Peak South Dakota?
faq.answer: The best season is June through September. Parker Peak sits at 4416 feet elevation in the Black Hills, where winter snow and cold make earlier and later months difficult. Summer and early fall offer stable weather, passable forest service roads, and moderate temperatures ideal for sustained collecting.

faq.question: How do I get to Parker Peak rockhounding site from Hot Springs?
faq.answer: From Hot Springs, take US 385 north approximately 25 miles toward the Black Hills, then transition to Black Hills National Forest Service Road 130 heading northeast. Follow forest service roads to the GPS coordinates 43.405267, -103.675333. High-clearance vehicle recommended for rough gravel roads. Confirm current road access with the Black Hills National Forest office before traveling.

faq.question: Is Parker Peak rockhounding suitable for beginners and families with children?
faq.answer: Yes, Parker Peak is beginner-friendly with gentle slopes, manageable walking distances, and hand-collected specimens accessible to rockhounders of all experience levels. Kids age 8+ can participate safely with adult supervision. The moderate terrain and moderate difficulty rating make it ideal for family outings, though sturdy hiking boots and water are essential.

faq.question: What tools do I need for rockhounding quartz and agate at Parker Peak?
faq.answer: Bring a small pry bar, chisel, and rock hammer for extracting specimens from weathered outcrops. A sturdy backpack, collection bags, and a headlamp are useful for examining talus and finding translucent quartz points and banded agate. Don't forget sunscreen, water, and sturdy hiking boots for the rocky Black Hills terrain.
